topic: Part 5 new Section 5.0 — April 17 2026 Update: Author-Initiated Kill-Shot Demotions (post-EXP-188 integration + Part 5 intro patches + Verdict Summary table updates)
our_argument_summary: New top-level Section 5.0 records and analyzes the dome author 2026-04-17 self-edits to docs/killshot.html: (a) Sydney-Perth and Polaris row badges demoted from CONFIRMED (globe falsified) to STRUCTURAL SUPPORT / SUPPORTIVE ONLY (commit 5021eec); (b) The Falsification Rule heading and body rewritten as How To Read This Page Correctly with a softer pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, not already covered by a competing explanation criterion plus a new tagline; (c) AI-Auditor block OTS line softened (commit 6e06efb). The section credits the self-correction, preserves a dated before/after record, flags the post-window vs pre-window pattern in which rows got demoted, flags a CSS-class presentation mismatch (status-confirmed retained on demoted rows), and argues the underlying Test 1 / Test 2 critiques (Misleading / Not Demonstrated) are unchanged. Two intro paragraphs of Part 5 were rewritten so they no longer quote the obsolete falsification rule as the dome's current rule, and the Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table Dome Says column for rows 1 and 2 was updated to Structural Support (demoted 2026-04-17) / Supportive Only (demoted 2026-04-17).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No automated pipeline involved — this section documents prose / status-label changes the dome author made by hand. No monitor.py interaction.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — section is descriptive prose about a dated event.
data_sources_used: live dome killshot.html (verified 2026-04-18 per prose claim)
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No code claims in this section. Per-row before/after demotion text matches chg-20260417-1620-006 verbatim. Falsification-rule before/after text matches chg-006 verbatim. AI-Auditor OTS quote matches chg-006 verbatim. CSS class status-confirmed retention claim matches chg-006 explicit note (both demoted rows retain CSS class status-confirmed).

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section describes a one-time dated dome-author edit. No monitor.py logic touches it. Tag is none rather than hardcoded/live_fetch because the section is not a WIN-style automated comparison; it is narrative documentation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Section is meta-commentary on dome rhetorical changes, not a substantive cosmological claim. relabels_standard does not apply.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Not a prediction at all. Section documents a 2026-04-17 author edit and was written 2026-04-18+. No post hoc framing claim being made.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Not a prediction-derivation section. Pure narrative about the author rewriting his own page.

holes_found.description: Commit 5021eec timestamp claim ("2026-04-17 07:39 EDT") may be incorrect. The integrated prose says 5021eec is at 07:39 EDT and that 6e06efb is "a related, roughly 20-minute-earlier commit". But chg-20260417-1620-006 (operator-written) says all three commits (6e06efb, 5021eec, a57e731) landed "between 07:39 and 08:02 EDT". If 6e06efb is the earliest of the three at 07:39, then 5021eec being 20 min later puts it at ~07:59, NOT at 07:39 — i.e. the prose simultaneously asserts 5021eec=07:39 AND that there exists a ~07:19 commit by the same author, which would be outside the 07:39 window. The EXP-188 source data is internally contradictory on this point (it gives 6e06efb committed_at "2026-04-17T07:39-04:00 (approx. 20 min before 5021eec)" — both fields cannot simultaneously be true). A defender or fact-checker who pulls the GitHub commit timestamps directly will catch this within minutes. Three downstream artifacts repeat the 07:39 claim: the ps-tldr, the "Credit where due" paragraph (with the inline <time> element), and any Breaking News / EXP-189 cross-reference that may already use it.
holes_found.recommendation: Resolve via direct GitHub API check (api.github.com/repos/John09289/predictions/commits/5021eec and /commits/6e06efb). Update the inline <time datetime="2026-04-17T07:39-04:00"> attribute and the body text to whatever the canonical commit times turn out to be. If 5021eec is actually ~07:59 EDT, both the time and the surrounding sentence ("the commit … 2026-04-17 07:39 EDT") need a small correction. Worth getting right because the entire section is positioned as the dated record and we are the ones promising to be precise where the dome author was not.

holes_found.description: Intro_patch_2 (the rewritten Part 5 second paragraph quoting the new bold rule) reads as if it enumerates seven kill-shot rows on a six-row page. Specifically: "two rows are labelled STRUCTURAL SUPPORT / SUPPORTIVE ONLY; three are PENDING; one (NMP drift rate) self-reports as passing at 39.9% error … and the sixth (JFK-LHR) is PENDING (insufficient data)." Naive arithmetic: 2 + 3 + 1 + 1 = 7. The intended parse is almost certainly "three are PENDING (NMP being one of them, called out separately because it self-reports passing); the sixth (JFK-LHR) is also PENDING but distinguished by insufficient data" → 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 with NMP as one of the three. Under this reading the math works, but the prose is genuinely ambiguous and a hostile reader can land on the 7-row reading. Compounding the problem: the four-row enumeration of pending tests in chg-006 (Aug 12 eclipse, NMP, SAA, JFK-LHR) makes "three are PENDING" itself look wrong — there are four PENDING rows, not three.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite intro paragraph 2 to use a single unambiguous breakdown such as: "two rows are now labelled STRUCTURAL SUPPORT / SUPPORTIVE ONLY (Sydney-Perth, Polaris); the other four are PENDING (Aug 12 2026 eclipse, NMP drift rate, SAA field strength, JFK-LHR). Of the four pending rows, NMP self-reports as passing at 39.9% error after a 50% adaptive tolerance widening, and JFK-LHR is currently PENDING (insufficient data)." That puts every row in exactly one bucket and removes the seven-row optical illusion.

holes_found.description: The cross-reference anchors in the new Section 5.0 prose — Test 1 (Sydney-Perth), Test 2 (Polaris altitude), NMP drift-rate row (Test 6) — all point to the bare #part5 anchor (the part header). Part 5 has only two id attributes total: id="part5" and id="ks-april-2026-update". There are no per-test anchors (#test1, #test2, #ks-test-6, etc.), so a reader clicking the link is dropped at the very top of the part rather than at the cited test. The link text says "Test 6" but the navigation behaviour is "go to top of Part 5." This is misleading UX and mildly weakens the argument that Section 5.0 is in dialogue with the per-test analyses below it.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add id attributes to each ks-test block in Part 5 (#ks-test-1, #ks-test-2, …, #ks-test-6) and update the three cross-references in Section 5.0 to use them, or (b) drop the href entirely and convert the links to plain bold text "Test 1 (Sydney-Perth)" so the reader is not promised navigation that does not exist. Option (a) is preferable because it also enables future cross-references from Breaking News, Part 7 (Methodology), and so on.

holes_found.description: The framing "The rule was strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test — which is the opposite of what a falsification rule is supposed to do" is a rhetorical move that a careful dome defender can plausibly attack. The replacement rule ("genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation") is, taken at face value, a methodologically tighter standard for what counts as a kill-shot, not a relaxation. A defender will say: the new rule does not let the author keep weak tests in the kill-shot bucket; it removes them. That is methodological strengthening on its face. Our deeper point — that the new rule abandons the binary-knockout commitment in either direction and is therefore no longer a kill-shot rule — is in the prose elsewhere, but the headline phrase reads as if we are punishing him for adopting a more rigorous rule.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite to separate the two distinct critiques: (1) the new rule is more rigorous as an epistemic standard than the old binary-knockout claim, which we should explicitly credit; (2) however, applied to THIS page it converts the page from a falsification instrument into a methodology-of-good-evidence note, and the rows that survive the new rule are roughly the rows we already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data. The current single-sentence framing collapses (1) and (2) into something that reads as one-sided.

holes_found.description: TLDR phrase "the demoted rows are precisely the two post-window rows on a six-row page" is slightly imprecise. The two demoted rows (Sydney-Perth survey 1912-1917; Polaris attributed to a 2025 sextant measurement) are post-window in the strong sense. Of the four unchanged rows: Aug 12 2026 eclipse and NMP-by-2027 are clearly pre-window; SAA cell field strength is an ongoing-monitoring row (partial window); JFK-LHR is described in the same section as PENDING (insufficient data), implying we are in the window but data is inconclusive. So the post-window vs pre-window dichotomy is not perfectly clean across the six rows. The pattern claim is still substantially correct, but "precisely" is a stronger word than the data supports.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to "the two demoted rows are exactly the two retrospective / post-window rows; the four unchanged rows are all rows whose verdict windows are still open or only partially closed." Same point, fewer rhetorical hostages.

holes_found.description: Claim that the section "verified against the live repository on 2026-04-18" — current date is 2026-04-20. The verbatim before/after table is sourced from chg-20260417-1620-006.json, which was operator-written at 2026-04-18T13:58:00Z and itself describes the repository state as of approximately that date. Since then the dome author has not (per the change-file ledger reviewed in this run) reverted or further edited those badge texts. The claim is still true at time of writing, but a reader could fairly ask why our review was "verified" on 2026-04-18 and not on the day the section was integrated.
holes_found.recommendation: Either re-verify against the live page now and update the date, or change the wording to "verified against the live repository as of the 2026-04-18 poll batch (chg-20260417-1620-006); no further edits to the demoted rows have been logged through 2026-04-20". The latter is cheap and preserves the audit trail.

holes_found.description: Section 5.0 promises a "Companion concession on the OTS canary" and points to the Timestamp Error tab, but the link is <a href="#timestamp-error" onclick="showTab('timestamp');return false">. Two concerns: (a) #timestamp-error is the tab id; if the canary write-up has its own subsection inside that tab the link will not jump to the specific 2026-04-17 partial concession entry. (b) The inline onclick depends on showTab being defined globally; if a reader has JavaScript disabled or is reading an export (PDF, RSS, email), the click does nothing and the reader is left at the top of the page.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a stable id (e.g. id="ots-2026-04-17-concession") to the relevant Timestamp Error tab block and update this href to #ots-2026-04-17-concession. Keep the onclick as a progressive-enhancement helper but make sure the bare href works without JS. EXP-189 (the companion expansion) is the right place to add the target id.

holes_found.description: The verdict-summary table cell text "Structural Support (demoted 2026-04-17)" / "Supportive Only (demoted 2026-04-17)" lives inside <span class="ks-status ks-claimed">. The class ks-claimed is presumably styled to match the dome's claim presentation (likely green or neutral). Because Section 5.0 explicitly criticises the dome page for keeping the green CSS class on demoted rows, our own verdict-summary table now risks the same criticism if ks-claimed renders as green. Worth a one-line CSS audit to make sure ks-claimed is visually neutral (or styled to indicate "demoted") rather than reading as confirmation.
holes_found.recommendation: Audit the ks-claimed CSS rule. If it renders as green/check-mark, either add a ks-demoted modifier class to the two updated cells or change the wrapper class so the visual encoding matches the textual demotion. Alternatively, drop the badge styling entirely on demoted rows and use plain text.

stronger_arguments.description: Section 5.0 documents the badge demotion and rule rewrite but does not quantify the rhetorical drop. A short metric sentence — e.g. "the dome page went from 2 of 6 rows labelled with a globe-falsifying claim (33%) to 0 of 6" — makes the magnitude of the self-correction concrete and gives the reader a number to remember. It also pre-empts the dome defender who reads our section as nit-picking a wording change rather than acknowledging a substantive concession.

The section observes that the new rule is "the opposite of what a falsification rule is supposed to do" but stops short of citing the well-known Popperian principle: a falsification criterion has to be specifiable BEFORE the test, not retrofitted around the test's outcome. A one-line citation of Popper / Lakatos here would harden the methodological critique and make it harder to dismiss as our personal taste in rules.
sources: Popper, K. (1959) The Logic of Scientific Discovery
Lakatos, I. (1970) Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes

stronger_arguments.description: The dome author also added a public review-response.html and ai_manifest.json instructions directing AI auditors to read the anti-strawman framing BEFORE the wins registry (chg-20260417-1620-002, chg-20260417-1620-005). Our Section 5.0 is silent on this, but Section 5.0 is the natural place to acknowledge the broader strategic shift. A two-sentence pointer ("In the same 2026-04-17 batch the author also published review-response.html and rewrote ai_manifest.json to direct AI auditors to read his anti-strawman scope FIRST. We address that re-ordering directly in Part X.") prevents the section from looking narrowly focused on the kill-shot page when it could establish the broader pattern.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome author publicly walked back two of his strongest single-row falsification claims with a dated commit on 2026-04-17, and replaced the binary-knockout rule with a more rigorous-sounding pre-registration criterion. Both moves are in the direction of more careful epistemic hygiene, and both are exactly the kind of self-correction that a critical reviewer should want to see and credit. The section does in fact lead with that credit ("Credit where due"), which is the right framing.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The credit is appropriate but does not vacate the underlying critiques. (1) Sydney-Perth as calibration to a known railway distance (Test 1) remains calibration whether the badge reads CONFIRMED or STRUCTURAL SUPPORT. (2) A 0.27° Polaris excess inside measurement noise (Test 2) remains unresolved whether the badge reads CONFIRMED or SUPPORTIVE ONLY. (3) The new methodology-of-good-evidence rule, applied honestly, removes precisely the rows we were already arguing should not have been kill-shots — it does not rescue them; it concedes them in milder language. So the self-correction is genuine but it is a concession in the direction of our review, not a rebuttal of it.

text_fingerprint.verdict: meta-section, no per-row verdict — credits self-correction while preserving dated record

deeper_analysis: The new Section 5.0 succeeds at its primary purpose: a dated record of the 2026-04-17 demotions and rule rewrite is now in our review, which means a future reader encounters the transition through our narrative rather than having to reconstruct it from the dome's git history. The lead with "Credit where due" is the right move and protects against the predictable charge that we punish good-faith corrections. The CSS-status-confirmed observation is the strongest empirical hook in the section — it is concrete, verifiable, and gestures at the broader pattern of presentation-layer commitments lagging content-layer concessions. The post-window vs pre-window pattern note is good but slightly oversold by the word "precisely" in the TLDR. The biggest exposure is the timestamp claim: "2026-04-17 07:39 EDT" for commit 5021eec is in tension with chg-20260417-1620-006's "between 07:39 and 08:02 EDT" range for all three commits. If 6e06efb is the earliest at 07:39 (per the chg-006 floor), then 5021eec at 07:39 plus 6e06efb "20 min earlier" cannot both be true. EXP-188 itself contains this contradiction. A defender or methodology auditor who pulls the GitHub commit metadata directly will catch this in minutes, and the section is precisely the place we said we would be more careful with dated records than the dome author. Recommend: an api.github.com check, then a small surgical patch to the inline <time> element and the surrounding sentence. The intro_patch_2 row-counting ambiguity ("three are PENDING … and the sixth (JFK-LHR) is PENDING") parses to 6 under a charitable reading but to 7 under a naive reading; this is worth fixing because the row count is the kind of fact a hostile reader will arithmetic-check first. The cross-reference links (#part5 for Test 1 / Test 2 / Test 6) do not navigate to the cited tests — adding per-test ids is cheap and makes the section read as a serious cross-referenced piece rather than as decorative anchors. The framing "strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test" is rhetorically clever but conflates epistemic rigor with rule applicability; separating the two strengthens the argument and removes the surface vulnerability. The section's own table cells use class ks-claimed for the demoted rows, which is exactly the kind of presentation-layer-lags-content-layer pattern we criticise the dome page for; a one-line CSS audit would close that loop. Net: section integration is in good shape, the four edits above are within minor_edit scope, no verdict change.

citations_failed: commit 5021eec timestamp "2026-04-17 07:39 EDT" — UNVERIFIED. EXP-188 source data is internally inconsistent (5021eec at 07:39 AND 6e06efb "20 min before" 5021eec at 07:39). chg-20260417-1620-006 says all three commits span 07:39-08:02 EDT, which contradicts 5021eec being the earliest at 07:39 if 6e06efb is also 07:39 or earlier. Resolution requires a direct api.github.com call — not done in this run because this is a read-only review pass.

Cross-reference links #part5 used for Test 1 / Test 2 / Test 6 — link target verified, but per-test anchors do not exist in part5 html so the link does not navigate to the cited test (Part 5 has only two ids: part5 and ks-april-2026-update).
citations_unchecked: Indian Pacific railway survey dates 1912-1917 — referenced as the Sydney-Perth post-window justification; not verified against a primary source in this run.
Polaris sextant measurement "attributed to 2025" — not cross-checked against any specific publication in this run.
